findings.title: Database connections not using TLS
findings.description: The CNPG_SIGMA1_URL in the sigma1-infra-endpoints ConfigMap uses a plain postgresql:// connection string with no sslmode parameter. CloudNative-PG supports TLS natively, but no subtask enables TLS on the cluster CR or appends ?sslmode=verify-full to connection strings. All six services would connect to PostgreSQL over unencrypted TCP within the cluster, exposing query data to any pod with network access to the databases namespace before CiliumNetworkPolicies are applied.
findings.recommendation: Add spec.postgresql.pg_hba entries enforcing hostssl and spec.certificates to the CNPG Cluster CR (subtask 1002). Append ?sslmode=verify-full to CNPG_SIGMA1_URL in the ConfigMap (subtask 1007). Mount the CNPG-generated CA certificate into each service pod.

findings.title: Valkey deployed without authentication
findings.description: Subtask 1004 explicitly states 'no auth for dev (document production auth requirement)'. However, no subsequent production-hardening subtask under Task 10 enables Valkey authentication. Any pod that bypasses Cilium policies (e.g., during rollout or misconfiguration) can read/write the Valkey instance containing rate-limit data, vetting result caches, and currency rate caches.
findings.recommendation: Enable requirepass on the Valkey CR and store the password in a dedicated Kubernetes Secret. Update all services' redis connection strings to include the password. The scale_task 10017 (Sentinel) should explicitly include auth configuration.

findings.title: JWT_SECRET shared as a single symmetric key across all services
findings.description: A single JWT_SECRET Kubernetes Secret is shared across equipment-catalog, finance, customer-vetting, social-engine, and Morgan. Compromise of any one service leaks the signing key for all services. There is no JWKS endpoint, key rotation mechanism, or per-service audience claim validation. No subtask specifies token expiry enforcement beyond checking the exp field.
findings.recommendation: Adopt asymmetric JWT signing (RS256/ES256) with a dedicated identity-issuing service or a JWKS endpoint. Each consuming service validates with the public key only. Add audience (aud) and issuer (iss) claim validation. Create a key rotation runbook alongside the existing secret rotation CronJob (subtask 10012).

findings.title: Internal/GDPR endpoints rely on weak defense-in-depth headers
findings.description: GDPR export and delete endpoints across all five services use different ad-hoc authentication mechanisms: equipment-catalog checks X-Internal-Request header (subtask 2010), RMS checks X-Internal-Key against an env var (subtask 3011), finance checks X-Internal-Key (subtask 4012), and customer-vetting has no specified internal auth (subtask 5007). This inconsistency means some GDPR endpoints are effectively unauthenticated and depend entirely on CiliumNetworkPolicy for access control. Network policy bypass (e.g., from Morgan pod compromise) would allow unauthorized data deletion.
findings.recommendation: Standardize on a signed internal service token (e.g., a short-lived JWT with 'internal' audience issued by the calling service) validated by all /internal/* endpoints. Alternatively, use mTLS client certificate verification (aligns with scale_task 10018). Document the chosen approach and enforce it consistently across all five services.

findings.title: Stripe webhook signature validation approach is ambiguous
findings.description: Subtask 4005 mentions validating Stripe-Signature via 'stripe-rust verify_webhook or manual HMAC-SHA256 computation with reqwest'. The Stripe signature verification is timing-sensitive and must use constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks. Leaving the implementation choice open risks a developer implementing naive string comparison. Additionally, the raw body must be preserved byte-for-byte before any deserialization — this is noted but not enforced at the framework level.
findings.recommendation: Mandate use of the stripe-rust crate's built-in webhook::Webhook::construct_event() which handles constant-time comparison internally. Add an explicit integration test that verifies a tampered signature is rejected. Add an Axum extractor that captures raw bytes before JSON deserialization.

findings.title: Signal-CLI uses :latest tag with no image pinning
findings.description: Subtask 1006 deploys Signal-CLI with image tag 'signalapp/signal-cli:latest'. This is mutable, meaning the image content can change between deploys. A supply chain attack on the Signal-CLI Docker Hub image would be automatically pulled into the cluster.
findings.recommendation: Pin to a specific Signal-CLI image digest (e.g., signalapp/signal-cli@sha256:...). Add the image to a container scanning pipeline. Consider mirroring the image to GHCR and scanning it with Trivy before cluster admission.

findings.title: No container image scanning task in CI/CD pipeline
findings.description: The CI/CD workflows defined in Task 10 include Semgrep, cargo-audit, nancy, and npm audit for dependency scanning, but there is no container image scanning step (e.g., Trivy, Grype) for the Docker images built for each service. Vulnerabilities in base images (debian:bookworm-slim for Rust, golang base for Go, node:20 for social-engine) would go undetected.
findings.recommendation: Add a container image scan step to the CI/CD pipeline (e.g., Trivy scan in security.yml or as a post-build step). Block deployment on CRITICAL/HIGH CVEs. The Dockerfile in subtask 2011 uses debian:bookworm-slim — consider distroless or alpine for reduced attack surface.

findings.title: No explicit SQL injection protection beyond parameterized queries
findings.description: While sqlx (Rust) and pgx (Go) use parameterized queries by default, subtask 2004 mentions 'build dynamic sqlx query using QueryBuilder' for the product search endpoint. Dynamic query building is a known SQL injection vector if string interpolation is accidentally used instead of bind parameters. Similarly, the social-engine (Node.js) uses postgres.js which supports tagged template literals but has no explicit mention of parameterized queries.
findings.recommendation: Add a lint rule or code review checklist item that prohibits raw string concatenation in SQL query construction. For the Rust equipment-catalog, ensure all QueryBuilder filter additions use .push_bind() exclusively. For the Node.js social-engine, mandate use of postgres.js tagged template syntax (sql`...`) and prohibit string interpolation in queries.

findings.title: ConfigMap contains database connection string with password reference
findings.description: Subtask 1007 stores CNPG_SIGMA1_URL in a ConfigMap (not a Secret). While the comment notes that the password is omitted from the URL and should be injected separately via PGPASSWORD, the pattern encourages putting connection metadata in a world-readable resource. A developer might later add the password to the ConfigMap URL for convenience, especially since the original task description (Task 1, item 7) shows postgresql://sigma1_user:$(PGPASSWORD)@... suggesting password inclusion was initially considered.
findings.recommendation: Keep the ConfigMap password-free as designed, but add a validating admission webhook or OPA/Gatekeeper policy that rejects ConfigMaps containing 'password=' patterns. Add a comment in the ConfigMap template explicitly warning against password inclusion.

findings.title: Morgan AI agent has unrestricted access to all backend services
findings.description: Morgan's CiliumNetworkPolicy (subtask 10009) allows egress to all 5 backend services. Morgan's MCP tools include sigma1_gdpr_delete which orchestrates DELETE calls to all services. If Morgan's model is tricked via prompt injection (a known LLM vulnerability), it could invoke data deletion tools without human confirmation. The admin skill prompt mentions a confirmation requirement, but this is enforced only in the LLM prompt, not at the API level.
findings.recommendation: Implement a human-in-the-loop gate at the API level for destructive GDPR operations: the sigma1_gdpr_delete tool should require a signed confirmation token generated by a separate approval workflow, not just an LLM prompt-level guard. Consider rate-limiting the /internal/gdpr/delete endpoints to 1 call per hour per customer_id. Add an audit log check that requires a matching sigma1_gdpr_export call before any delete is permitted.

findings.title: No Pod Security Standards or admission controller configured
findings.description: No task or subtask mentions Kubernetes Pod Security Standards (PSS), Pod Security Admission (PSA), or OPA/Gatekeeper/Kyverno policies. Without these, pods could run as root, mount host paths, or use privileged security contexts. The Signal-CLI pod in particular may require elevated permissions for its functionality, but this is not explicitly scoped.
findings.recommendation: Apply the 'restricted' Pod Security Standard at the namespace level for sigma1, databases, and openclaw namespaces. Create exceptions only where required (e.g., Signal-CLI may need specific capabilities). Add securityContext to all Deployment templates: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false.

findings.title: No centralized audit trail for security-sensitive operations
findings.description: While individual services log GDPR deletions to an audit schema, there is no centralized security event logging for authentication failures, authorization denials, rate limit triggers, or CiliumNetworkPolicy drops. The Prometheus alerting rules (Task 10, item 10) cover performance metrics but not security events.
findings.recommendation: Create a dedicated audit log table in the audit schema for security events (auth_failures, rate_limit_exceeded, gdpr_operations). Configure structured logging (JSON) across all services with a security_event field. Add Loki alert rules for: >10 auth failures per minute per source IP, >5 GDPR delete operations per hour, CiliumNetworkPolicy DROP events exceeding baseline.

findings.title: PII stored unencrypted at rest in PostgreSQL
findings.description: The vetting_results table stores business verification data, LinkedIn profile data, Google Reviews data, and credit scores (subtask 5002). The crew_members table stores names, emails, and phone numbers (subtask 3003). The payroll_entries table stores worker names and payment amounts (subtask 4002). None of these tables use column-level encryption, and the CNPG cluster CR does not specify encryption at rest via spec.storage.encryption.
findings.recommendation: Enable CNPG storage encryption (spec.storage.pvcTemplate with storageClass using encrypted volumes). For highly sensitive fields (credit_score, email, phone), implement application-level encryption using pgcrypto's pgp_sym_encrypt with a key stored in a separate Kubernetes Secret, or use a Key Management Service integration.

findings.title: File upload endpoints lack content-type validation and size limits
findings.description: The social-engine's POST /api/v1/social/upload (subtask 6004) accepts multipart file uploads but does not specify content-type validation (only JPEG/PNG should be accepted), maximum file size limits, or file content scanning. Malicious files could be uploaded to R2 and later served to other users or processed by sharp (which has had CVEs related to malformed image files).
findings.recommendation: Validate Content-Type and magic bytes of uploaded files against an allowlist (image/jpeg, image/png). Set a maximum file size (e.g., 20MB per file, 100MB total per request) in Elysia's body parser configuration. Run uploaded files through sharp metadata extraction before full processing to catch malformed files early.

findings.title: CORS configuration is permissive in development with no production tightening plan
findings.description: Subtask 7007 configures Morgan web chat CORS with cors_origins: ['*'] and notes 'tighten to Sigma-1 domain post-launch'. The Rust equipment-catalog (subtask 2002) uses CorsLayer described as 'permissive for dev'. No production task explicitly updates these to restrictive origins.
findings.recommendation: Add a subtask under Task 10 to audit and tighten all CORS configurations. Set specific origins matching the production website domain and mobile app deep link scheme. For the Morgan web chat, restrict to the exact Cloudflare Pages domain.

findings.title: Dependency versions not fully pinned for Node.js/TypeScript services
findings.description: Subtask 6001 installs packages like 'elysia@1.x' and 'effect@3.x' using major version ranges. While this is common in Node.js, it means npm/bun install could pull different minor/patch versions between builds, potentially introducing supply chain vulnerabilities.
findings.recommendation: Use a lockfile (bun.lockb or package-lock.json) committed to the repository to ensure reproducible builds. Add npm audit / bun audit to the security.yml CI workflow. Consider pinning exact versions in package.json for production services.

findings.title: ArgoCD has broad ClusterRoleBinding but no restrictions on accessible repos
findings.description: Subtask 10011 mentions a ClusterRoleBinding for the ArgoCD service account. ArgoCD's default ClusterRole grants cluster-admin equivalent access. No mention of restricting ArgoCD to specific namespaces or limiting the Git repositories it can sync from.
findings.recommendation: Configure ArgoCD with project-scoped RBAC restricting sync targets to sigma1, databases, openclaw, and signal namespaces only. Use ArgoCD Projects to limit allowed source repositories and destination namespaces.

findings.title: No incident response runbook for security breaches
findings.description: The ops/runbooks/ directory is created for secret rotation only. There is no runbook for responding to a security incident such as a compromised API key, unauthorized data access, or DDoS attack.
findings.recommendation: Create ops/runbooks/incident-response.md covering: 1) API key compromise (immediate rotation steps), 2) Unauthorized GDPR deletion (audit log review and data restoration), 3) Morgan prompt injection (tool access revocation), 4) Pod compromise (network isolation via Cilium, forensic log collection).

strengths: CiliumNetworkPolicies provide genuine zero-trust network segmentation with per-service allow-listing — this is significantly above average for early-stage platforms
GDPR compliance is built into every service from day one with dedicated export/delete endpoints and audit trail logging, rather than being retrofitted
Rate limiting is implemented at the application layer (Valkey sliding window) rather than relying solely on infrastructure, providing per-tenant/IP throttling

Each database service uses schema-level isolation with per-service PostgreSQL roles (sigma1_catalog, sigma1_rms, etc.) having USAGE grants only on their respective schemas, enforcing least-privilege at the data layer
The CI/CD pipeline includes multi-tool security scanning (Semgrep, cargo-audit, nancy, npm audit) with merge-blocking on CRITICAL/HIGH findings
Secrets are properly externalized into Kubernetes Secrets rather than embedded in ConfigMaps or application code, and a rotation reminder mechanism exists
Background vetting pipeline wraps each external API call in individual timeouts with graceful degradation, preventing a single external service failure from hanging the entire pipeline
summary: The plan demonstrates strong foundational security patterns including zero-trust networking, GDPR-by-design, and multi-layer CI security scanning. However, it has high-severity gaps in transport-layer encryption (unencrypted Postgres and Valkey connections), authentication architecture (shared symmetric JWT key with no rotation), and inconsistent internal endpoint authentication across services. The Morgan AI agent's unrestricted access to destructive GDPR tools without API-level human confirmation gates represents a significant prompt injection risk. Addressing the 5 high-severity findings before production launch is essential.
